Team,

 

Fred and I met last week to discuss areas for improvement in our forecasting process. I plan to meet with Tom this week to review a few ideas, after which Fred and I will regroup and prepare recommendations for the broader team.

 

In the meantime, if you’d like to explore different forecasting approaches, here is a useful blog post: 12 proven sales forecasting methods to predict revenue | Outreach

 

Thank you,
MA

 

Marc Alex Forget

Senior Vice-President of Sales and Marketing | Vice‑président principal, Ventes et Marketing

437-500-2700
aptum.com | LinkedIn

From: Frederic Gingras <Frederic.Gingras@aptum.com>
Sent: Tuesday, February 24, 2026 8:19 PM
To: Ian Rae <Ian.Rae@aptum.com>; Sarah Blanchard <Sarah.Blanchard@aptum.com>; Adam Nelson <Adam.Nelson@aptum.com>; Marc Alex Forget <Marc-Alexandre.Forget@aptum.com>; Marc Pare <Marc.Pare@aptum.com>
Cc: Tom Kelsch <Tom.Kelsch@aptum.com>; Jorge Quintero <Jorge.Quintero@aptum.com>
Subject: Thoughts on improving forecasting

 

Hi team,

 

I've been thinking about our forecasting calls, how the forecast changes so much between the start and end of the month, and I've been collecting some thoughts around this.  Keen to hear your thoughts...

 

@Marc Alex Forget, the following may or may not apply in your case, as I understand that defining timelines with partners involved can be... complex... but keen to get your feedback on this.

 

 

 

We often see:

  • Opportunities in wrong stages
  • Close dates that are wrong
  • Deals constantly slipping

 

Issue 1: Since the stages are pretty well defined, then there is an issue with the understanding of the rep of each stage. It is most likely attributable to either slowness to change (stages were slightly different before, especially with legacy Aptum stages), or they haven't gone through understanding the sales stages thoroughly (just skimmed over).

 

 

Remediation: FG to go through sale stages definition thoroughly with the team.

 

 

Issue 2: I repeated multiple time the importance to tie the close date to the sequence of event, what needs to happen before signature, and how it fits in the customer process.  Yet, not much changes.

 

If you can't answer these questions, the deal cannot be in a later stage, not can it be closing this month: 

  • When is the customer making the decision?
  • Who signs?
  • What happens if it doesn’t get approved this month?
  • What priority is this compared to other projects?

I already told the team "if you don't have a timeline defined with the customer, push it".  If customer is "hoping", don't put it this month.

 

 

Remediation: No evidence = No commit

 

For a deal to sit in the current month commit, it must have:

  • Identified Economic Buyer
  • Confirmed Decision Criteria
  • Documented Decision Process
  • Mutually Agreed Timeline
  • Next meeting scheduled

Yes, I'm wondering if I should bring back MEDDPICC... or part of it, at least.

If the Economic Buyer, Decision criteria and Paper process are not identified, then you cannot have a close date this month, or you cannot move to stage 4... to be discussed. 

 

 

Issue 3: pushing close date...

In addition to what I covered earlier, with tying the close date to specific timeline/sequence of events, some date slippage can be inevitable (shouldn't be the majority).  Things change and shift at customers. 

 

We have "close lost" reasons/details, what if we have "Close date push details" ?

MA and I explain pushed opportunities weekly on the forecast call, which requires constant awareness and knowledge of the opportunities progress. What if we could have this in an easier way?

 

 

Remediation: Enforce "Why is it pushed"

What if we make it structured.  Not as a free-text (excuse) box.

If close date moves:

  • Required field: Reason for push
    • Budget not approved
    • Economic buyer not engaged
    • Legal delay
    • Competing priority
    • No confirmed timeline
    • Rep error (which forces accountability)

We can then review patterns monthly.

And then we can orient the coaching around the pattern.

 

Granted, this is limited details.  Maybe there is a field for details...

 

 

I don't want to make this more complicated, but I think we need to bring more evidence and make the reps more accountable.

 

 


Frederic Gingras

Sr Director, Account Management

514-913-9153

aptum.com | LinkedIn

 

Book time to meet with me
